data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Maritime raider disruption of bronze supply chains caused political-military spirals in metal-dependent states", "mechanism": "The Sea Peoples and analogous groups disrupted Cypriot copper and tin trade routes; states with bronze-dependent military hierarchies (Mycenaean, Hittite, Ugaritic) faced recursive crises as inability to equip chariot forces undermined extraction capabilities, leading to internal rebellion and fragmentation; Egypt's bronze dependency was mitigated by Nubian gold-for-copper trade alternatives and by its ability to absorb raiders into existing military structure", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Mesopotamia (bronze-independent military)"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Hittite army was not chariot-based bronze military but iron-equipped mass infantry", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Cyprus copper production uninterrupted throughout 1200-1150 BC with no trade route disruption evidence", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egypt suffered equivalent political fragmentation from same supply disruption", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Economic interdependence vulnerability; Susan Sherratt's 'consumption crisis' variant; emphasizes systemic feedback rather than single invasion"}
